source.definition: An adverb used to emphasize truth or actuality, meaning 'in fact' or 'truly'.

explanation.sourceText: The English word 'really' comes from the adjective 'real', which derives from the Latin 'realis' meaning 'actual' or 'genuine'. It developed into an adverb to express emphasis on truth or reality.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „really” provine de la adjectivul „real”, care derivă din latină, din „realis”, însemnând „actual” sau „autentic”. A evoluat într-un adverb pentru a exprima accentuarea adevărului sau realității.

target.definition: Adverb, das 'wirklich' oder 'tatsächlich' bedeutet und zur Verstärkung dient.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian adverb 'chiar' derives from the Latin 'cārius', originally a comparative form of 'carus' (dear, valuable), and evolved into an adverb of emphasis.
explanation.targetText: Adverbul românesc „chiar” provine din latină, de la 'cārius', comparativul lui 'carus' (scump, valoros), evoluând într-un adverb de întărire.

learningTips.text: Remember 'chiar' sounds like 'key-ar'—think of a key that really unlocks something important.
The English word 'really' and Romanian 'chiar' both emphasize truth—think of 'really real' and 'chiar adevărat'.
The Romanian 'chiar' comes from Latin 'cārius', meaning 'more dear'—so it adds value or emphasis, like saying 'really valuable'.
When you say 'really' in English and 'chiar' in Romanian, both add strength to your statement—like saying 'this is really important'.
